title: Membership Operators
content:
### Membership Operators

Membership operators check if a value **exists** in a sequence (string, list, tuple, set, dictionary).

---

### `in` and `not in`

| Operator | Example | Returns True When |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| `in` | `x in y` | `x` is found in `y` |
| `not in` | `x not in y` | `x` is NOT found in `y` |

---

### Checking in Lists

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print("banana" in fruits)    # True
print("grape" in fruits)     # False
print("grape" not in fruits) # True
```

### Checking in Strings

```python
text = "Hello, World!"
print("Hello" in text)      # True
print("hello" in text)      # False (case-sensitive)
```

### Checking in Dictionaries

`in` checks **keys** by default:

```python
data = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25}
print("name" in data)       # True
print("Alice" in data)      # False (checks keys, not values)
```

---

### Checking in Tuples and Sets

```python
t = (1, 2, 3)
print(2 in t)  # True

s = {1, 2, 3}
print(4 in s)  # False
```

---

> **Performance Note:** For large collections, `in` is **fast** on sets and dictionaries (O(1)) but **slower** on lists and tuples (O(n)).
codeExample:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print("banana" in fruits)
print("grape" not in fruits)

text = "Hello, World!"
print("World" in text)

user = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25}
print("age" in user)
